perturbation.original_text: Telnet hereby covenants to pay all invoices rendered to its account by Galaxy within 15 days of the date of invoice; Interest shall accrue on overdue accounts at the rate of 1.5% per month calculated and payable from the date of invoice until the date of payment in full of the overdue amount.
perturbation.changed_text: Telnet hereby covenants to pay all invoices rendered to its account by Galaxy within 60 days of the date of invoice; Interest shall accrue on overdue accounts at the rate of 25% per month calculated and payable from the date of invoice until the date of payment in full of the overdue amount.
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the payment terms, extending the payment period to 60 days and drastically increasing the monthly interest rate to 25%, which contradicts usury laws. These laws regulate the maximum interest rate that can be charged on a debt.
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states have usury laws that limit the amount of interest that can be charged on loans and other debts. Charging 25% per month would likely violate these laws in most jurisdictions. 12 U.S. Code § 85 allows national banks to charge interest rates allowed by the state where the bank is located, but this doesn't remove state-level protections for non-bank entities.

Rate of interest on loans, discounts and purchases Any association may take, receive, reserve, and charge on any loan or discount made, or upon any notes, bills of exchange, or other evidences of debt, interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the State, Territory, or District wh
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the interest rate to 25% per month, which is likely to violate state usury laws. The Perturbation Explanation explicitly states this change 'contradicts usury laws' that regulate the maximum interest rate. The Law Explanation also states that charging 25% per month would likely violate these laws in most jurisdictions. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'interest at the rate allowed by the laws of the State'. Since the changed text specifies an extremely high interest rate compared to the original text (25% vs 1.5%), this change constitutes a clear violation of state usury laws and creates a significant legal contradiction, as it violates a legally mandated standard for interest rates.

perturbation.original_text: Thereafter, this agreement will renew automatically from year to year unless cancelled in writing by either Party giving the other written notice of such cancellation a minimum of 60 days before the end of the then current term.
perturbation.changed_text: Thereafter, this agreement will renew automatically from year to year unless cancelled in writing by either Party giving the other written notice of such cancellation a minimum of 15 days before the end of the then current term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cancellation notice period to 15 days may violate state laws requiring longer notice periods for automatic renewals in certain contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17602
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17602 requires that for automatic renewal contracts, businesses must provide clear and conspicuous disclosure of the automatic renewal terms, and that consumers have the right to cancel the contract within a certain period. Shortening the notice period for cancellation to 15 days might be deemed insufficient and could violate the spirit of this law, particularly if the initial contract term is lengthy.

Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 9. Automatic Purchase Renewals [17600 - 17606] ( Article 9 added by Stats. 2009, Ch. 350, Sec. 1. ) 17602. (a) It is unlawful for any business that makes an automatic renewal offer or continuous service offer to a consumer in this state to do any of the following: (1) Fail to present the automatic renewal offer terms or continuous service offer terms in a clear and conspicuous manner before the subscription or purchasing agreement is fulfilled and in visual proximity, or, in the case of an offer conveyed by voice, in temporal proximity, to the request for consent to the offer. If the offer also includes a free gift or trial, the offer shall include a clear and conspicuous explanation of the price that will be charged after the trial ends or the manner in which the subscription or purchasing agreement pricing will change upon conclusion of the trial. (2) Charge the consumer’s credit or debit card, or the consumer’s account with a third party, for an
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cancellation notice period from 60 to 15 days, which contradicts the purpose of California Business and Professions Code Section 17602. The law aims to ensure consumers are clearly informed about automatic renewal terms and have sufficient time to cancel. As stated in the provided snippet: "It is unlawful for any business that makes an automatic renewal offer or continuous service offer to a consumer in this state to do any of the following: (1) Fail to present the automatic renewal offer terms or continuous service offer terms in a clear and conspicuous manner..." A 15-day notice might be deemed insufficient to allow consumers to act on the information, thus undermining the legal requirement for clear disclosure and reasonable opportunity to cancel. The reduction of the notice period directly impacts the consumer's ability to exercise their cancellation right effectively, establishing a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All covenants of confidentiality herein shall survive the term of this agreement by three (3) additional years counting from the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All covenants of confidentiality herein shall survive the term of this agreement by fifty (50) additional years counting from the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 50 years could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under certain circumstances, potentially conflicting with laws or legal principles that disfavor overly broad or perpetual restrictions on competition and information flow.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 187 states that a promise to refrain from competition that imposes a restraint that is unreasonably long is unenforceable. While this pertains to non-compete agreements directly, the principle applies analogously to confidentiality agreements. A 50-year restriction on confidential information, especially in rapidly evolving industries, may be deemed unreasonably long and therefore unenforceable as against public policy promoting competition and innovation. (See Para 26 of the second link for an instance where this citation was used.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 3-year confidentiality period post-termination, while the changed text extends this to 50 years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that such an extended period could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, conflicting with legal principles against overly broad restrictions on competition and information flow. The Law Explanation supports this by referencing Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 187, which states that a promise to refrain from competition that imposes an unreasonably long restraint is unenforceable. Even though the scraped snippets are empty, the legal principle stated in the law explanation supports the determination that the modification creates a legal contradiction by potentially being deemed unreasonably long and unenforceable.
